content: I'm porting my example kernel module over to Rust for Linux infrastructure and I may be mistaken but I think it's impossible to currently implement an out of tree build for char devices. PointerWrapper is not exported so when you try to define file operations on the device, you can't specify the type using kernel::. Want me to put up a PR exporting PointerWrapper?
PointerWrapper is a trait implemented by Box<T> and Ref<T>

Yes, and the definition here: https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/kernel/file/trait.Operations.html requires PointerWrapper to be explicitly specified. This can be accomplished within kernel:: using crate:: but for out-of-tree builds, this trait is undefined because it is not exported from kernel::.

I'm not sure why you think you need to mention PointerWrapper. See the examples in samples/rust-- none of them are part of the kernel crate, they don't mention PointerWrapper and yet they implement file::Operations just fine
Ok this seems to be a style difference! I was writing in terms of Self::Data, not the concrete type.
John Baublitz has marked this topic as resolved.

Well, if this is confusing to people, I'd like to understand it better.

Could you share a snippet of your code that required PointerWrapper to be mentioned?

I was specifying the data parameter as _data: <Self::Data as PointerWrapper>::Borrowed<'_> instead of &AtomicUsize. This is a stylistic guideline we follow on my project so that we only need to change data types in a single place.

Oh, I see... Perhaps we should export PointerWrapper then.

Although I find &AtomicUsize to be quite an improvement in readability over <Self::Data as PointerWrapper>::Borrowed<'_>, it does have the drawback that you describe: it would require more changes to replace it with something else
I think in this case it's definitely more readable. I don't have a strong opinion either way if this is generally the style we go for.
The thing is that we're taking the choice away from people, and we're also preventing them from implementing PointerWrapper for their own types
Anyway, if you want to send a PR exporting PointerWrapper, go ahead and I'll take it
Ok will do!

Still working on this. I've been using a VM on a laptop that heavily throttles CPU usage since it has no fan :sad: I'm currently working on setting my development environment up on a cloud machine for faster compile times because it seems like Make is unfortunately rebuilding a lot pretty often.

Do you mean e.g. modifying the kernel crate that triggers a rebuild of all the Rust code, or something worse?

In case it helps to know if your setup is taking too long: in an "average" CPU, I can rebuild the kernel crate and all samples in ~30 seconds with 3 jobs.
I mean that running make LLVM=1 triggered an entire kernel rebuild for some reason after I made the edit.
it is probably an error on my end, but given how long it takes on that VM, I'm going to switch to a more powerful machine just in case it happens again.

FWIW, when compiling the kernel to run with busybox on a minimal VM, you can configure it with make allnoconfig qemu-busybox-min.config rust.config (instead of defconfig), and you'll get smaller kernel. You will of course eventually want to build with defconfig but this will save some time during development. The builds the kernel + rust libs in under a minute on my machine
okay I'll keep that in mind! for now the problem seems to be resolved but running it on a much larger machine seems to speed it up.
John Baublitz has marked this topic as unresolved.
Okay, here's the PR! https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/814
John Baublitz has marked this topic as resolved.
